text:
Admission Date:  1970-10-29       Discharge Date:  1974-06-12

Date of Birth:   2018-01-03       Sex:  M

Service:  Cardiothoracic

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  Mr. Armstrong is a 64-year-old
male who presented with complaints of substernal chest pain
and chest pressure over the last three months.  Work up
ultimately revealed symptomatic aortic stenosis, although he
had normal coronary.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  Hyperlipidemia, mild hypertension.  He
has no history of diabetes.  No history of stroke, no history
of myocardial infarction, no coronary disease.  Cardiac
catheterization had been performed as an outpatient and at
time this procedure revealed mild to moderate aortic stenosis
with a peak gradient of 31 mmHg, borderline left ventricular
hypertrophy.  There was a bicuspid valve noted.  Valve area
was approximately 0.7 cm squared and otherwise there was
clean coronary arteries.

OUTPATIENT MEDICATIONS:
1.  Lopressor 25 mg po bid
2.  Lipitor 10 mg po q day
3.  Aspirin 325 mg po q day

ALLERGIES:  No known drug allergies.

DENTAL EXAM was done prior to the operation and required no
tooth extraction.

FAMILY HISTORY:  Noncontributory.

SOCIAL HISTORY:  He is a retired electrical engineer.  He has
no tobacco history.  He has an occasional glass of wine with
dinner.  Lives with his wife, has no children.  Walks 3 to 4
miles per day.

The patient was ultimately referred by Dr. Donna Molina
for the outpatient cardiac catheterization.  This is the
patient's cardiologist and after discovery of his symptomatic
AS, he was referred to Dr. Joshua [**Last Name (Prefixes) **] for aortic valve
replacement.

PREOPERATIVE LABS:  Hematocrit of 39.8, BUN of 17, creatinine
value of 1.0.  He had a PT value of 12, INR value of 1, PTT
of 27.5.

IMAGING:  He had a preoperative chest x-ray that showed
normal radiographic appearance of his chest and no acute
cardiopulmonary disease.  His baseline electrocardiogram
revealed sinus bradycardia at a rate of 57 and he had delayed
precordial R-wave progression.  No previous tracings were
available for comparison.

HOMartinez COURSE:  With these findings, the patient was sent
to the Operating Room as a same day admission on 1970-10-29.
With the assistance of Dr. Benjamin Williams, Dr. [**Last Name (Prefixes) **]
performed an aortic valve replacement utilizing a 22 mm
Fischer-Nelson valve.  The patient's pericardium was left
open.  He left the Operating Room with a right arterial line,
a right IJ Swan-Ganz catheter.  He had two ventricular and
two atrial pacing wires.  He had a mediastinal and a left
pleural chest tube.  Coronary artery bypass time was 292
minutes with a crossclamp of 198 minutes.  His mean arterial
pressure was 100 with a CVP of 11.  He left the Operating
Room in sinus rhythm just on nitroglycerin at 0.5 mcg per kg
per minute.  Postoperatively, he was noted to have some
relative hypoxia and a postoperative film showed a left
pneumothorax.  Therefore, a left chest tube was placed along
the fourth intercostal space in a mid to anterior axillary
line which, with re-expansion, had improvement of his
pulmonary parameters.

By postoperative day #1, he had a low grade temperature of
100.5??????, .............. 63 and in sinus rhythm.  His blood
pressure was 110/52.  He had CVPs of 6.  He was still
intubated after having the hypoxia issues which would have
been improved with his chest tube.  His chest tube was noted
to have put out 760 cc in a 24 hour period.  He additionally
received 6 units of packed cells and gotten a total of 14
liters in over the first 24 hours postoperatively.  He was
weaned and extubated on postoperative day #1 and was kept NPO
for the time being.  It should be noted that the patient's
postoperative ejection fraction was 55%.  Postoperative
hematocrit was 27.4 with a BUN and creatinine of 14 and 0.9.

By postoperative day #2, the patient had had some bursts of
atrial fibrillation and had been started on Lopressor and
amiodarone.  He was otherwise awake and alert, following
commands.  Chest was clear.  He had a sternal wound that was
intact without drainage.  His hematocrit was 26 with a BUN
and creatinine of 23 and 0.9.  His diet was serially
advanced.  Hematocrits were stable and monitored.  The
patient was up out of bed and walking, working with physical
therapy and doing well.

By postoperative day #3, he was on the floor.  His rate was
better controlled in the 80s.  His chest tubes were removed.
His wires were also removed and his Foley catheter was
discontinued at this time.  Given the fact that he had been
in atrial fibrillation for almost a 48 hour interval and had
intermittent bursts of atrial fibrillation, it was relatively
rate controlled.  His anticoagulation was thereafter started
postoperatively at the recommendations of the Dr. Donna
Anthony Nguyen of the electrophysiology service.  It should be
noted that on postoperative day #5 the patient had spiked a
temperature to 101.6?????? and at that time he was also
complaining of increasing pain and fatigue and worsening
diarrhea and some vague abdominal symptoms.  A chest x-ray
had been done which had shown some slight increased pulmonary
vascularity suggestive of possible pulmonary edema, left
greater than right.  His hematocrit was 25.8.

At this time, he was given intravenous Lasix.  His
hematocrits were falling serially.  He was continued on
Lopressor and his doses were titrated.  He was on an oral
amiodarone regimen.  A urinalysis was sent.  He was pan
cultured otherwise and a Clostridium difficile analysis was
sent as well.  By postoperative day #6, his pain persisted in
his left abdomen and he had persistent high volume diarrhea.
A general surgery consult was obtained and empiric Flagyl was
started.  His hematocrit at this time was 29 and he was
being diuresed adequately.  After a general surgery
consultation was obtained with Dr. Benjamin Webb,
recommendations were to repeat all of his stool cultures, to
empirically treat for Clostridium difficile.  The patient
would ultimately require an outpatient colonoscopy.  After
some other concerns were expressed about possible biliary
colic in terms of having vague abdominal pain that was poorly
localized, an ultrasound was obtained which showed sludge,
but no wall thickening, no pericholecystic fluid and no
sonJohn Reyes signs of acute cholecystitis.  His liver function
tests were otherwise unremarkable.

Ultimately, blood cultures, urine cultures and Clostridium
difficile x3.  Assays were negative for this patient.  His
diarrhea serially improved.  He did have one episode of
bloody diarrhea during this time, however his hematocrit
remained stable.  At the time of discharge, the patient was
undergoing three to four bouts of loose stools per day, but
had been markedly improved.  He was without nausea or emesis
and his abdominal pain was essentially vanished.  His wounds
were stable.  He was afebrile.  He was in sinus rhythm at
this time on an amiodarone and Lopressor regimen for rate
control and also for rhythm control.  He was being
heparinized and ultimately his INR was greater than 1.7 and
therefore he was cleared for discharge to home.

At the time of discharge, postoperative day #10, he was on
Flagyl day 5 of 10, ambulating at level 5 and had cleared
stairs without assistance.  He was afebrile with T-max of
98.8??????, pulse of 68 in sinus rhythm, blood pressure of 124/72
with a respiratory rate of 20 and he was saturating at 95% on
room air.

His exam was notable for clear lungs bilaterally.  His heart
was regular with a slight click.  He had no murmurs.  Abdomen
was soft, nontender, nondistended.  At this time, he had
normoactive bowel sounds.  Extremities were non edematous or
warm.  Given this clinical presentation, he was cleared for
discharge.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
1.  Coumadin 5 mg po q day with a PT/INR check to be done on
1970-08-21 and the results will be faxed to Dr. Donna
Molina.
2.  Lopressor 75 mg po tid
3.  Flagyl 500 mg po tid x5 days
4.  Amiodarone 400 mg po tid x48 hours, then to change to 400
mg po bid x1 week, then to change to 400 mg po q day x1 week,
then to change to 400 mg po q day x1 week and ultimately took
200 mg po q day.
5.  Lipitor 10 mg po q day
6.  Percocet 5/325 1 to 2 tablets po q 4 to 6 prn
7.  Colace 100 mg po bid
8.  Aspirin 325 mg po q day

The patient can have a visiting nurse assistant with PT/INR
checked.  Additionally, he will need [**Initials (NamePattern4) **] Martinez-of-Hearts monitor
so the results can be reviewed by his cardiologist, Dr.
Mason.  This was set up prior to the patient's
discharge.  He will be on a cardiac heart healthy diet as
tolerated.

FOLLOW UP:  See Dr. Martinez in approximately two to three
weeks from the time of discharge so that the review of his
gastrointestinal symptoms can be undertaken, as well as he
can be set up for an outpatient colonoscopy given his
diarrhea and heme positive stools during admission.
Additionally, he will follow up with Dr. Donna Molina
in approximately one week where his Coumadin therapy can be
serially adjusted as well as his medication list can be
reviewed and he should see Dr. Joshua [**Last Name (Prefixes) **] in
approximately two to three weeks from time of discharge.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:
1.  Status post aortic valve replacement with a 23 mm
Fischer-Nelson valve complicated by postoperative
fibrillation, postoperative diarrhea/colitis.
2.  Hyperlipidemia
3.  Hypertension
4.  Prior history of asymptomatic aortic stenosis with
borderline left ventricular hypertrophy




                            Williams [**Last Name (Prefixes) 413**], M.D.  36097

Dictated By:Vasquez
MEDQUIST36
